# **![][image1]**

# **Review Summary**

The Auditware team has performed a security review on apps.fun.

Apps.fun is a protocol that enables token creators to launch AMM pools with virtual  
reserves for initial liquidity bootstrapping. The protocol implements a two-stage liquidity  
model where tokens start in custom Curve pools with virtual reserves and graduate to  
Uniswap V4 positions once they reach a WETH threshold.

The following items were in scope for the audit:

* Smart contract vulnerabilities in contracts/evm/src/  
* Access control and centralization risks  
* Liquidity manipulation and graduation process security

The following items were explicitly defined out of scope:

* Known behaviors documented in "Trust Assumptions" and "Known Limitations" sections  
* Third-party dependencies (OpenZeppelin, Uniswap V4)  
* Issues requiring compromised private keys  
* Vulnerabilities in the testnet deployment or test tokens

Audit commit hash: [**535d2**](https://github.com/numbergroup/app.fun/commit/535d2c84513b869a1964d8fca6d1cfce0b658042)

## 

## Contract Overview

### Protocol Trust Assumptions

The contract operates under these assumptions:

* The contract trusts that ERC20 tokens behave correctly. Malicious tokens (e.g., tokens with transfer hooks, blacklists, or admin functions) can cause unexpected behavior or fund loss.    
* Users are responsible for verifying token contract addresses and implementations before trading.    
* Virtual reserves are set at pool creation and affect pricing calculations.    
* The owner has significant control over protocol parameters including virtual reserves, graduation thresholds, and fee recipients.    
* Uniswap V4 PositionManager behaves correctly and positions can be created and managed as expected.

### Protocol Accepted Risks

It's important to note that there are high-risk design limitations not mentioned in this report, as they were classified as protocol accepted risks, such as:

* Virtual reserves create a pricing discrepancy where pools appear more liquid than they actually are. Early swaps benefit from this discrepancy, which is an intentional feature for bootstrapping.    
* The owner can change critical parameters including virtual base reserve, graduation threshold, and fee recipient addresses without timelock. Users must trust the owner.    
* Graduation can be triggered by anyone when the WETH threshold is met. There is no access control on the graduation function.    
* Contract addresses can be creators, which may cause issues with fee claiming if the contract doesn't implement proper receive functions.    
* Direct token transfers to Curve contracts can affect reserve calculations, though sync() can correct this.

We recommend re-visiting those risks as soon as possible in the protocol's lifetime, and until then to **notably warn users** and ensure they understand and acknowledge these risks before wiring funds through the protocol.

# **Findings Overview**

| Finding | Component | Risk Level |
| :---: | :---: | :---: |
| **[AW-H-01: Poisoned Position Denial of Service](#aw-h-01:-poisoned-position-denial-of-service)** | FeeHolder | ***High*** |
| **[AW-M-01: Owner Centralization Risk](#aw-m-01:-owner-centralization-risk)** | Access Control | ***Medium*** |
| **[AW-M-02: Curve Re-initialization](#aw-m-02:-curve-re-initialization)** | Curve | ***Medium*** |

# **AW-H-01: Poisoned Position Denial of Service** {#aw-h-01:-poisoned-position-denial-of-service}

**Severity:** High									**Status:**Unmitigated

**Locations:**

* [**contracts/evm/src/FeeHolder.sol\#L174-L178**](https://github.com/numbergroup/app.fun/blob/535d2c84513b869a1964d8fca6d1cfce0b658042/contracts/evm/src/FeeHolder.sol#L174-L178)

**Description:**

The \`claimLPFees()\` function loops through all positions for a token and attempts to claim fees from each. If \`\_claimLPFees()\` reverts for any position, the entire function reverts, blocking fee claims for all positions of that token.

A poisoned position can be created through several scenarios:

\* Token with malicious transfer logic that reverts    
\* Uniswap V4 position that's been closed or modified externally    
\* Token that doesn't implement ERC20 correctly    
\* Position that requires special handling that fails

This creates a denial-of-service attack vector where one bad position can prevent all creators from claiming fees from valid positions for that token.

**Recommendation:**

* Add error handling in the loop to continue processing other positions even if one fails:

  for (uint256 i \= 0; i \< positions.length; i++) {

        uint256 positionId \= positions\[i\];

        if (positionCreator\[positionId\] \!= msg.sender) continue;

        try this.\_claimLPFees(positionId) {} catch {

            // Log error but continue

            emit FeeClaimFailed(positionId);

        }

    }

# **AW-M-01: Owner Centralization Risk** {#aw-m-01:-owner-centralization-risk}

**Severity:** Medium								**Status:**Unmitigated

**Locations:**

* [**contracts/evm/src/AppsFun.sol\#L78-L105**](https://github.com/numbergroup/app.fun/blob/535d2c84513b869a1964d8fca6d1cfce0b658042/contracts/evm/src/AppsFun.sol#L78-L105)

**Description:**

The owner has significant control over the protocol. Without timelock or multi-sig protection, the owner can:

* Change \`virtualBaseReserve\` (affects new pools)  
* Change \`graduationThreshold\`  
* Change \`feeHolder\`, \`positionManager\`, \`platformFeeAddress\`  
* Change \`owner\` itself  
* Withdraw all liquidity from any pool via \`withdrawalLiquidity()\`

While fee percentages are constants and cannot be changed, the owner can redirect platform fees to their own address, prevent graduation by setting a very high threshold, or break the system by changing critical addresses to malicious contracts.

**Recommendation:**

* Implement a transparent timelock for critical owner functions  
* Use multi-sig for owner  
* Consider making some parameters immutable after deployment  
* Document owner powers clearly to users

# **AW-M-02: Curve Re-initialization** {#aw-m-02:-curve-re-initialization}

**Severity:** Medium								**Status:**Unmitigated

**Locations:**

* [**contracts/evm/src/Curve.sol\#L56-L69**](https://github.com/numbergroup/app.fun/blob/535d2c84513b869a1964d8fca6d1cfce0b658042/contracts/evm/src/Curve.sol#L56-L69)

**Description:**

The \`Curve.initialize()\` function can be called multiple times because there's no initialization guard. The factory (AppsFun owner) can reset \`token0\`, \`token1\`, \`virtualReserve0\`, and \`virtualReserve1\` at any time.

While the creator mapping in \`getPair\[token\]\` cannot be changed, reinitializing a curve could reset virtual reserves, affecting pricing calculations for existing pools. The owner could deploy a contract that calls \`Curve.initialize()\` with new parameters, potentially manipulating pool behavior.

**Recommendation:**

* Add an initialization guard to prevent re-initialization:


    bool private initialized;

    function initialize(...) external {

        require(msg.sender \== factory, "AppsFun: FORBIDDEN");

        require(\!initialized, "AppsFun: ALREADY\_INITIALIZED");

        initialized \= true;

        // ... rest

    }

 of the contents of this report and does not make any guarantees that following the advice   
within will prevent security incidents or issues.
